questions.q: What is the formula for power using voltage and current?
questions.e: P = V × I. Power equals voltage multiplied by current.

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, what is the same across all branches?
questions.e: Voltage is the same across all parallel branches. Current splits.

questions.q: In a series circuit, what is the same throughout?
questions.e: Current is the same throughout a series circuit. Voltage splits.

questions.q: Formula for resistance using Ohm's Law?
questions.e: R = V ÷ I. Ohm's Law triangle: V top, I and R bottom.

questions.q: Formula for current using Ohm's Law?
questions.e: I = V ÷ R.

questions.q: Series circuit with 4Ω, 6Ω and 10Ω — total resistance?
questions.e: Series: R = 4 + 6 + 10 = 20Ω.

questions.q: Parallel circuit total resistance is always?
questions.e: Always less than the smallest individual branch resistance.

questions.q: Parallel circuit: 6Ω and 12Ω in parallel — total resistance?
Power formula using current and resistance?
questions.e: P = I² × R.

questions.q: Power formula using voltage and resistance?
questions.e: P = V² ÷ R.

questions.q: Current drawn by a 2300W appliance on 230V?
Resistance of an appliance drawing 3A at 230V?
Resistance increases in a metal when?
questions.e: Resistance increases with temperature and length; decreases with larger cross-sectional area.

questions.q: Class II appliance — what does it rely on?
questions.e: Class II = double insulated. No earth required. Symbol: square within a square.
Class I relies on earthed metalwork and the earth conductor.
SELV — max 50V AC or 120V DC ripple-free.
Separated Extra Low Voltage — isolated from earth and all other circuits.

o: PELV connected to earth; SELV isolated from earth
questions.e: PELV = connected to earth. SELV = isolated from earth and all other circuits.

questions.q: Standard RCD personal protection trip current?
questions.e: 30mA personal protection. 100mA or 300mA for fire and equipment protection.
RCBO = RCD + MCB. Protects against both earth faults and overload.
Overload or short circuit. Earth leakage is detected by an RCD.

questions.q: Voltage drop limit for a power circuit?
questions.e: 5% power, 3% lighting.

questions.q: Voltage drop limit for a lighting circuit?
questions.e: 3% lighting. 5% power.

questions.q: Most common UK domestic earthing arrangement?
questions.e: TN-C-S (PME). Combined PEN conductor splits at the cut-out.

questions.q: TN-S earthing — where does the earth come from?
questions.e: TN-S — earth cable from the armoured cable below the supplier's fuse.
TT — overhead cables to remote properties. Own earth rod driven into ground at premises.
10mm² from earth bar to all metallic services.
6mm² linking appliances within a zone. Clamps to BS951 on clean bare metal.

o: A plastic fitting interrupts metallic pipe continuity
questions.e: Plastic fittings break metallic continuity. 6mm² cable bridges either side.
Two crocodile clips + ~1.5m of 10mm² yellow/green earth cable.

questions.q: When must temporary continuity bonding be used?
o: Before cutting or removing any metallic pipework or pump
questions.e: Fit either side of any cut, pump removal or radiator removal to maintain earth continuity.

o: Identify what is being worked on
questions.e: Step 1: Identify what is being worked on.

questions.q: When do you prove the voltage indicator during safe isolation?
o: Before AND after testing the dead circuit
At the start of the working day only
questions.e: Prove before and after testing the dead circuit — steps 3 and 5.
GS38 = approved, insulated test leads. Max 4mm exposed tip, preferably 2mm.

questions.q: Non-contact voltage indicators are suitable for safe isolation?
o: No — not approved for safe isolation
questions.e: Non-contact voltage indicators are NOT suitable for safe isolation.
Post-2004: Brown = Line, Blue = Neutral, Green/Yellow = Earth.
Old colours: Red = Live, Black = Neutral, Green = Earth.

questions.q: Which wiring system is fire resistant for extreme environments?
questions.e: MICC — Mineral Insulated Copper Clad. Fire resistant.

o: Underground or where mechanical protection needed
questions.e: Steel Wire Armoured — underground or where mechanical protection is needed.

questions.q: Standard site voltage for portable tools?
questions.e: 110V yellow. Step-down transformer required.

o: Lighting in damp or confined spaces
questions.e: 25V violet — lighting in damp or confined spaces.

o: Connects metalwork to prevent voltage differences
questions.e: Bonds all metalwork to prevent dangerous voltage differences between them.

questions.q: What does a fuse do that an MCB cannot?
o: Melt to break circuit — single use
questions.e: Fuse melts — single use. MCB is resettable.
Live touches neutral — fuse blows or MCB trips.

questions.q: Lighting circuit — cable size and MCB rating?
questions.e: Lighting: radial circuit, 1.5mm² cable, 6A MCB.

questions.q: Ring main — cable size and MCB rating?
questions.e: Ring main: 2.5mm² cable, 32A MCB. Max plug fuse 13A.

questions.q: Which appliances have their own dedicated radial circuit?
questions.e: Showers, immersions, cookers and macerators each have their own MCB-protected radial circuit.

questions.q: Danger zone for Part P electrical work?
questions.e: Danger zone = bathroom and outhouse only. Kitchen was removed from the danger zone.

questions.q: Why check manufacturer's instructions before working on a macerator?
o: Capacitors hold charge after unplugging — risk of shock
questions.e: Capacitors hold charge. Always check MIs for macerators, microwaves and heating pumps.

o: A = W ÷ V then round up
questions.e: Watts ÷ Volts = Amps, then round up to nearest fuse size (3A, 5A, 13A).
16A MCB, radial circuit, double pole isolator switch.

questions.q: Supplementary bonding clamps must be fitted to?
questions.e: BS951 clamps on clean bare metal — no paint.

questions.q: PAT testing on site — how often typically?
questions.e: Typically every 3 months on construction sites. Green = pass, red = fail.

questions.q: How is electricity generated by heat?
questions.e: Heat applied to two dissimilar metals creates voltage. Used in thermocouple gas valve pilot flame.

questions.q: Heating controls — what does the zone valve end switch do?
o: Sends signal to fire boiler and start pump when valve fully open
questions.e: End switch closes when zone valve reaches fully open — signals wiring centre to fire boiler and run pump.
4.187 kJ/kg·K. Used in Q = m × c × ΔT.

o: Change of state with no temperature change
questions.e: Change of state — no temperature change during the transition.
Sensible heat changes temperature. No change of state.
9.81 kPa. Approximately 10 kPa for practical purposes.
10 kPa = 1m head = 0.1 bar.
101.325 kPa at sea level.
Absolute = gauge + atmospheric (101.325 kPa).
Gauge pressure is measured above atmospheric pressure.

questions.q: Static vs dynamic pressure — which is higher?
questions.e: Static (water at rest) is always higher than dynamic (water flowing).

questions.q: Pascal's Law states pressure in a liquid acts?
questions.e: Equally in all directions. Unlike solids which only exert downward pressure.
P1V1 = P2V2. Pressure and volume inversely proportional at constant temperature.
V1/T1 = V2/T2. Volume and temperature directly proportional at constant pressure.

o: Gases are compressible; liquids are not
They behave the same under pressure
questions.e: Gases are compressible and expand to fill their container. Liquids are incompressible.
Expansion = original length × coefficient of expansion × temperature rise.

questions.q: Copper pipe 4m long, 50°C rise, coeff 0.000017. Expansion?

o: Zinc leaching from brass in hard water
questions.e: Zinc leaches from brass leaving a porous copper structure. Use DZR fittings in hard water areas.

o: Two dissimilar metals in contact with an electrolyte
questions.e: Two dissimilar metals + electrolyte (water). One acts as anode and corrodes.

o: Contains iron (rusts) vs no iron (corrosion resistant)
Used only for fittings vs pipework
Does not conduct electricity vs does
questions.e: Ferrous = iron, rusts. Non-ferrous = no iron, corrosion resistant.

o: Can be drawn into wire or bent without breaking
questions.e: Ductile = drawn into wire or bent without breaking. Copper is ductile.
Malleable = shaped by hammering. Lead is malleable.

o: Ability to be drawn into wire
questions.e: Resistance to being pulled apart.

o: Forces acting across the material in opposite directions
questions.e: Resistance to forces acting across the material — like a bolt being cut sideways.

o: Toughness = impact resistance; hardness = surface indentation resistance
Hardness = impact resistance; toughness = surface resistance
questions.e: Toughness = absorbs impact without fracturing. Hardness = resists surface indentation or scratching.
1000 kg/m³ = 1 kg per litre.
Volume = mass ÷ density.
RD < 1 = floats. RD > 1 = sinks.
20°C typical for Low Pressure Hot Water systems.

questions.q: Why is water ideal for LPHW heating systems?
o: High specific heat capacity — stores and carries large amounts of heat
It does not expand when heated
questions.e: High SHC (4.187 kJ/kg·K) means water stores and transports heat efficiently.
Maximum density at 4°C.
Freezes and expands by 10%.

questions.q: Water heated from just above 0° to just below 100° — expansion?
questions.e: Expands approximately 4% when heated through this range.
Expands 1600× its original volume. Hence why safety devices are critical.
0°C = 273K. Add 273 to convert Celsius to Kelvin.
Absolute zero = −273°C = 0K. No molecular movement.
Below 7 = acidic. Above 7 = alkaline. 7 = neutral.
Temporary hard = calcium carbonates. Releases scale above 65°C.

o: Calcium sulfates — cannot be removed by boiling
questions.e: Permanent hard = calcium sulfates. Cannot be softened by boiling.
Plumbo solvency = water's ability to dissolve lead. Origin of the word 'plumber'.
Cupro solvency = water aggressive towards copper. Occurs in very soft or acidic water.

questions.q: Water softeners use which material to remove hardness?
questions.e: Zeolite/resin bed removes calcium and magnesium ions. Regenerated by brine solution.

o: Scale inhibitors do NOT soften — they prevent crystal binding
questions.e: Scale inhibitors break down salt crystals to prevent binding but do NOT soften water.
RD 0.58 — lighter than air. Disperses upward when leaked.
RD 1.78 — heavier than air. Pools in low areas.
RD 2.0 — heavier than air. Pools in low areas.

questions.q: Natural gas air:gas ratio for combustion?
questions.e: Natural gas 10:1. Propane 24:1. Butane 30:1.
Butane 121.5 MJ/m³. Propane 95.0. Natural gas 38.5.
Natural gas 1930°C. Propane 1980°C. Butane 1996°C.

questions.q: Which gas is too hot for soldering?
questions.e: Butane at 1996°C is too hot. Natural gas at 1930°C is not hot enough.
Conduction (molecule to molecule), convection (fluid/gas), radiation (infrared).
Copper is the best thermal conductor. Still air is the worst.

questions.q: What percentage of heat output from a panel radiator is convection?
questions.e: Approximately 90% convection through fins, 10% radiation.

questions.q: Dark colours vs light colours for heat absorption?
questions.e: Dark/matt colours absorb heat. Light/shiny/polished colours reflect heat.

questions.q: Capillary action in a smaller tube?
questions.e: Smaller diameter tube = greater capillary rise.

o: Atmospheric pressure difference between two vessels at different heights
questions.e: Lower vessel has greater atmospheric pressure — water transfers from high vessel to low vessel.

questions.q: Manometer — where do you read the water level?
questions.e: Always read from the bottom of the curve (meniscus).

questions.q: Magnetite in a heating system is caused by?
questions.e: Air + iron + water = rust = magnetite (black sludge).

questions.q: Hydrogen in a heating system indicates?
o: Corrosion occurring — iron reacting with water
questions.e: Hydrogen produced by corrosion. Can be bled from radiators. Never ignite to test.

questions.q: Force of water — 40 litres in a system?
questions.e: Force = mass × 9.81. 40 litres = 40kg. 40 × 9.81 = 392N.

questions.q: Reducing pipe diameter has what effect on velocity and pressure?
o: Increases velocity, decreases pressure and flow rate
questions.e: Smaller diameter = faster velocity but lower pressure and flow rate.

questions.q: Equivalent pipe length of a tight 90° elbow?
questions.e: 0.37m equivalent. Machine bend = 0.3m. Check valve = 2.5m.

o: Thermosetting sets permanently; thermoplastic can be re-melted
Thermoplastic sets permanently; thermosetting can be re-melted
questions.e: Thermosetting = permanent set. Thermoplastic = softens and can be reshaped when heated.

o: Increased turbulence from burrs or poor workmanship
questions.e: Turbulence from burrs left after cutting accelerates erosion. Always deburr after cutting.

o: Localised attack confined to a small area
questions.e: Localised corrosion in a small area — can penetrate through pipe wall while surrounding metal appears fine.
Report within 15 days (day of accident excluded, weekends included).
Over-3-day: record only. Over-7-day: report within 15 days. Deaths: immediately.
1.2m. Deeper: batter sides at 45°, bench, or shore up.

questions.q: Spoil minimum distance from excavation edge?
questions.e: 1 metre — fixed, not depth-dependent.
Never exceed 15 PSI. Working pressure 5–8 PSI.
Risk = Likelihood × Consequence (each 1–5, max 25).
13–25 = High. Stop work.

o: Medium — do not stop work but take immediate action to mitigate
questions.e: 6–12 = Medium. Do not stop work but take immediate action to mitigate.
8 steps.
Blue (Crocidolite) most dangerous fibres. Brown (Amosite) most dangerous in board form.
Full ban 1999. Blue/brown banned 1985. Most white banned 1993.

o: Stop, withdraw, report, licensed contractor only
questions.e: STOP → WITHDRAW → REPORT → Licensed contractor only.
30–40 years latency period.

questions.q: Asbestos disease caused by scarring of lung tissue?
questions.e: Asbestosis = scarring of lung tissue.

o: Below ground or in boiler rooms
questions.e: Never below ground or in boiler rooms. Heavier than air — pools.

questions.q: Minimum distance between LPG and oxygen storage?
questions.e: 3m minimum or separated by a 30-minute fire wall.
Acetylene = left-hand thread. Oxygen = right-hand thread.
Flashback arresters must be fitted on BOTH lines.

o: Shut both valves immediately; do not use until inspected
questions.e: High-pitched whistle + flame burning back. Shut both valves immediately.
Wet chemical only. Never water or CO₂ on cooking oil.

questions.q: CO₂ extinguisher covers which fire classes?
questions.e: CO₂ covers B (liquids), C (gases) and E (electrical).
A B C E — best universal choice for plumbers.

o: Pull, Aim at base, Squeeze, Sweep
questions.e: Pull → Aim at base → Squeeze → Sweep. Stand 3m back.

o: Not an offence to ignore but evidence of guilt if prosecuted
questions.e: Not an offence to ignore but is evidence of guilt if prosecuted.

questions.q: HSE Improvement Notice — minimum time to comply?
questions.e: Minimum 21 days. Can be appealed.
Immediate work stoppage. Cannot continue until notice is lifted.

o: Hazard = anything that may cause harm; Risk = chance of harm occurring + severity
Hazard = injury sustained; Risk = cause of injury
Risk is always the higher value
questions.e: Hazard = anything that MAY cause harm. Risk = CHANCE of harm + severity.

o: Maximum 4 rungs above the tie point
questions.e: 75°. 4 units up, 1 unit out from base.

questions.q: Ladder must be tied if taller than?
questions.e: Tie if over 3m. Extend 1m above stepping-off point. Belt buckle between stiles.

questions.q: Indoor tower scaffold maximum height ratio?
questions.e: 3.5× shortest base indoors. 3× outdoors. Tie to building above 6.4m.

questions.q: Tower scaffold — when must it never be moved?
o: When people or materials are on it
questions.e: Never move with people or materials on the scaffold.

o: Run under cold water for 10 minutes; no cream; do not burst blisters
questions.e: 10 minutes cold water. No cream or ointment. Do not burst blisters.
100–120/min at 4–5cm depth. 30:2 ratio.
Never touch if still in contact. Isolate supply first or use dry non-conducting material.

o: Wash loose particles with clean water — do not remove penetrating objects
questions.e: Wash loose particles only. Never attempt to remove penetrating objects.

o: Take reasonable care for self and others
questions.e: Employees take reasonable care for themselves and others.
S.2: safe systems of work and training.
Avoid → Prevent → Minimise.
Upper action level 85 dB(A). Lower = 80 dB(A). Exposure limit = 87 dB(A).
Yellow triangle = warning or danger.
Blue circle = mandatory action (e.g. must wear hard hat).

questions.q: Red circle with diagonal bar means?
questions.e: Red circle + diagonal bar = prohibition.
EN 20345 — steel toecap and midsole.

questions.q: Hard hat standard and replacement rule?
o: BS EN 397 — replace annually or after impact
questions.e: BS EN 397. Replace annually or after any impact.
EN 374 for chemical gloves (acids, solvents, fluxes).

o: Avoid where possible; kinetic technique if unavoidable
questions.e: Avoid → plan route → use mechanical aids → kinetic technique if manual handling unavoidable.
H&S file stays with the building until demolition.

questions.q: Permit to Work vs Method Statement?
o: PTW = formal written authorisation for high-risk work with named persons and declaration on completion
questions.e: PTW = formal authorisation, names persons, sets time frame, requires written declaration on completion.

questions.q: Active flux (acid-based) — what must you do after soldering?
o: Wash off thoroughly inside and out — causes pitting corrosion if left
questions.e: Active flux must be cleaned off completely inside and out — causes pitting corrosion if left.

o: Well-ventilated area, short periods, no naked flames
questions.e: Very strong fumes. Well-ventilated area, short periods, keep away from naked flames.

questions.q: Hot works — when can you leave site?
o: As soon as flame is extinguished
At least 1 hour after completing hot works
questions.e: Finish hot works at least 1 hour before leaving. Check for smouldering before replacing floorboards.

questions.q: Confined spaces — if someone collapses inside?
o: Never enter after someone overcome — call emergency services
questions.e: Never enter after someone overcome — you become a second victim. Call emergency services.
Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations. Equipment must be inspected, marked with SWL, used by competent persons.

questions.q: R220 copper pipe — what is it used for?
o: Soft, thick wall, coils — underground and microbore
questions.e: R220 = fully annealed (soft), thicker wall. Coils, underground, microbore heating.

o: Half-hard — most common above-ground plumbing and heating
questions.e: R250 = half-hard. Standard above-ground plumbing and heating in 3m straight lengths.

questions.q: R290 copper pipe — why rarely used in UK?
o: Hard temper, thin wall — not suitable for bending
questions.e: R290 = hard temper, thin wall. Will not bend. Rarely used in UK.
BS EN 1057 = copper tubes. R220, R250, R290.
Blue = wholesome cold water. Yellow = gas. White = central heating. Green = other water.

questions.q: Chrome-plated copper pipe — why can't you use push-fit?
o: Grippers will not grip chrome surface
questions.e: Grippers cannot grip the chrome surface. Use compression or clean chrome and solder.

o: Amount subtracted from measured length to get correct cut length
Additional length for bends in LCS
questions.e: Pipe gain = subtract from measured length. Pipe appears to get longer through a bend.
~4mm. 22mm copper at 90° ≈ 6mm.

questions.q: Bend radius for copper pipe calculation?
questions.e: Bend radius = pipe diameter × 4.
Scissor bender — R250 half-hard 15mm and 22mm copper. Main domestic/workshop tool.
Microbore ball bender — R220 soft copper 6, 8 and 10mm.

questions.q: LCS bending differs from copper because?
o: Pipe stretches approximately one nominal bore each side of the mark — deduct from measurement
questions.e: LCS bends in both directions from mark. Stretches ~nominal bore each side. Deduct from centre-to-centre.

o: Distance from pipe end to centre of fitting — deducted from centre-to-centre
questions.e: X dimension = pipe end to fitting centre. Deducted from centre-to-centre when calculating cut length.

questions.q: Maximum notch depth in a joist?
questions.e: Maximum 1/8 of joist depth. Top of joist only.

o: Top, 0.1–0.2 of span from each support
questions.e: Top only. Zone: 0.1 to 0.2 of span from each support.

questions.q: Maximum hole diameter in a joist?
questions.e: 1/4 of joist depth. Must be on neutral axis (middle third of depth).
0.25 to 0.4 of span from support. Middle third of joist depth.
3 × hole diameter minimum spacing.

questions.q: Joist notches vs holes — key position rule?
o: Notches near supports; holes towards mid-span
questions.e: Notches: 0.1–0.2 from support. Holes: 0.25–0.4 from support (further toward mid-span).

questions.q: PTFE on compression fittings — correct or not?
o: No — the olive provides the seal
questions.e: Never use PTFE on compression fittings. The olive does the sealing.

questions.q: Type A vs Type B compression fitting — key difference?
o: Type A for plastic; Type B for copper
Type A — olive seals, accessible location; Type B — pipe end flared, suitable for burial
Type B uses PTFE; Type A does not
Type A underground; Type B above ground
questions.e: Type A = olive, accessible. Type B = flared pipe end, can be buried.

o: Cleans and prevents oxidation so solder bonds
questions.e: Flux de-oxidises metal so solder flows and bonds properly.

o: Pre-loaded solder — heat until ring appears at mouth
questions.e: Solder ring = pre-loaded solder. Heat until ring appears. Flux still required.
99% tin, 1% copper. Mandatory for potable water systems.

questions.q: Lead solder — when is it still acceptable?
o: Heating, gas and non-potable systems only
questions.e: Lead solder (70% lead, 30% tin) for heating, gas and non-potable only.
Silicone spray only. Active flux corrodes metal grippers and joint fails.

questions.q: Push-fit on plastic pipe — what insert is required?
questions.e: Pipe insert prevents the pipe wall collapsing under compression.
Apply cement to pipe only, not fitting. Push in and twist. Insoluble once set.

questions.q: Electrofusion welding (MDPE) — during the weld cycle?
o: Do not disturb — melts wire coil fusing surfaces
questions.e: Do not disturb during cycle. Wire coil melts and fuses pipe and fitting surfaces together.

questions.q: DZR fittings — when are they required?
o: Hard water areas to prevent dezincification
questions.e: Hard water areas. Stamped CZ or DZR. White powder on brass = dezincification occurring.
Not suitable for hot water. Max approximately 60°C.

o: It returns to its original straight shape when heated
It returns to round section when heated — useful for push-fit
questions.e: Shape memory = returns to round when heated. Useful for push-fit connections.

questions.q: Barrier pipe — why essential for heating?
o: Oxygen barrier prevents oxygen ingress which causes rust and magnetite
questions.e: Without barrier, oxygen permeates through plastic pipe walls causing corrosion of ferrous components.
MDPE blue = cold water mains underground. Degrades in sunlight — must be buried.

questions.q: Polypropylene pipe — can it be solvent welded?
questions.e: Polypropylene cannot be solvent welded. Slightly greasy feel. Recognisable by appearance.
15mm copper: 1200mm horizontal, 1800mm vertical.
15mm plastic: 600mm horizontal, 1200mm vertical. Much closer than copper.

questions.q: Why does plastic pipe need more clips than copper?
o: Greater risk of sagging and more thermal movement
questions.e: Plastic has more thermal expansion and greater tendency to sag.
D2 discharge pipe must be clipped every 300mm.

questions.q: Horizontal chase in a wall — maximum depth?
questions.e: Horizontal: max 1/6 of wall thickness. Not back-to-back — must be staggered.

questions.q: Vertical chase in a wall — maximum depth?
questions.e: Vertical: max 1/3 of wall thickness. Not back-to-back on same course.

questions.q: Why must copper pipe in screed be plastic-coated?
o: Lime in concrete is highly corrosive to copper
questions.e: Lime in concrete attacks copper. Plastic-coated sleeve protects it.

questions.q: Purpose of a pipe sleeve through a wall?
o: Prevents pipe rubbing, prevents lime/cement attack, allows thermal movement
questions.e: Sleeve sealed at both ends. Prevents rubbing, protects from lime/cement, allows movement.

questions.q: Lagging strip between pipe and joist — purpose?
o: Prevents expansion creaking as pipe heats and cools
questions.e: Flat hair felt strip prevents the pipe rubbing against timber during thermal movement.

questions.q: LCS grades — Heavy, Medium, Light — what is different?
o: Same OD — heavier grade has thicker wall and smaller bore
questions.e: All same OD. Heavier grade = thicker wall = smaller internal diameter.
BSPT — British Standard Pipe Thread, tapered.

questions.q: Hemp and jointing compound — where is it NOT suitable?
o: Potable water and gas — not suitable
questions.e: Hemp + paste = heating only. Not potable water or gas. PTFE for those.

questions.q: LCS union fitting — when is it used?
o: Where two fixed points need joining — allows disconnection without moving pipes
questions.e: Flat-face union allows pump or component removal without moving the surrounding pipework.

questions.q: Trace heating — what does it do?
o: Low-temperature element on pipe prevents freezing and can overcome dead legs
questions.e: Thermostat-controlled low-temp element on pipework. Prevents freezing; overcomes active dead legs.

questions.q: Lead pipe joining — what fitting is used?
o: Lead lock proprietary compression fitting — no wipe joints permitted
questions.e: Lead lock fitting only. Wipe joints are not permitted.

questions.q: WRAS/RAS approval — what does it mean?
o: Legal requirement for fittings used on wholesome water systems
questions.e: WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) approval = legal requirement for wholesome water fittings.

questions.q: Soundness test for copper/steel — pressure held for?
questions.e: Hydraulic test at 1.5× working pressure held for 1 hour. No pressure drop acceptable.

questions.q: Plastic pipe Test B — acceptable result?
o: Total pressure loss less than 0.2 bar over 120 minutes
questions.e: Test B: total pressure loss less than 0.2 bar over the 120-minute test period.

questions.q: After soundness testing — what must you do?
o: Flush system until water runs clear
questions.e: Flush system. Open all taps until water runs clear. Disinfect only if system dormant or large commercial.

questions.q: Chipboard floor — how do you cut an access opening differently from floorboards?
o: Frame out all four sides of any opening cut
questions.e: Frame out all four sides with timber noggins. Unlike floorboards which only need ends supported.

o: HDPE is older black pipe largely superseded by MDPE
MDPE is older; HDPE is newer
HDPE is used for gas; MDPE for water
questions.e: HDPE is the older black pipe largely superseded by MDPE.

questions.q: Press-fit joints — can they be removed?
questions.e: Press-fit = one-time use. Cannot be removed. Mark insertion depth before pressing.
Clerk of Works — ensures work meets agreed plans and standards on behalf of the client.

questions.q: CDM 2015 — when is a project notifiable?
o: 30+ days AND 20+ simultaneous workers, OR 500+ person-days
questions.e: Notifiable: 30+ days AND 20+ simultaneous workers, OR 500+ person-days.

o: Formal written instruction for scope change — client must sign before work proceeds
questions.e: Formal written, client-signed BEFORE work proceeds.

o: Controls costs, produces bills of quantities, values variations
questions.e: QS: controls costs, bills of quantities, values variations, interim payments.

o: Communication has only worked when it has been understood
questions.e: Communication has only worked when it has been understood.

o: Step-by-step safe system of work produced after risk assessment
questions.e: Step-by-step safe system of work. Produced after risk assessment. Required at tendering.
Operatives must sign RAMS before work begins.

questions.q: Delivery note — does it contain pricing?
o: No — supplier, date, quantity, description, order reference only
questions.e: Delivery note contains no pricing. Supplier, date, qty, description, order ref only.

o: Planned sequence, timing and duration of work programme
questions.e: Work programme — coordinates trades, sets sequence, monitors progress.

o: Defects identified at end of job, rectified before handover
questions.e: Defects at completion. Rectified before handover.

o: Final record of what was actually installed
questions.e: Final record of what was actually installed — may differ from design drawings.
Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service. Free employment mediation.

questions.q: First fix vs second fix plumbing?
o: First fix = appliances; second fix = pipework
First fix = pipework before plastering; second fix = appliances after decoration
First fix = drainage; second fix = hot and cold
questions.e: First fix = pipework before plastering. Second fix = appliances and connections after decoration.

questions.q: Equality Act 2010 — how many protected characteristics?
questions.e: 9 protected characteristics including age, gender, race, disability, religion.

o: Building regulations compliance checks at key stages — local authority representative
questions.e: Building Control Officer — local authority. Checks building regs compliance at key stages.

o: Carry out work as instructed; report problems; cannot authorise variations
questions.e: Level 2: carry out work as instructed. Cannot authorise variations.
Formal quotations must always be in writing.

o: Quotation = fixed price; estimate = may vary
questions.e: Quotation = fixed price. Estimate = flexible, may vary.

o: Right to know what data is held about you and that it is handled correctly
questions.e: Right to know what personal data is held and that it is handled correctly.

o: Right to access information from public bodies
questions.e: Right to access information held by public bodies.

o: Advisory — enforceability comes from legislation referencing them
Mandatory on public sector projects only
questions.e: BS Standards are advisory. They become enforceable when referenced by legislation.

questions.q: Manufacturer's instructions — can they override BS Standards?
o: Yes — MIs take precedence where they conflict with BS Standards
questions.e: Manufacturer's instructions take precedence over BS Standards where they conflict.
5 × 5 = 25 maximum risk score.

questions.q: CDM 2015 H&S file — when does it get destroyed?
o: Never — stays with building until demolition
questions.e: H&S file stays with the building until demolition.

o: Manufacturer's instructions, benchmark certificate, warranties, contact numbers, demonstration to customer
questions.e: Manufacturer's instructions, benchmark certificate, warranties, contacts, and demonstration of system operation.

o: Converts client brief into design and leads management team
questions.e: Architect converts client brief into design. Leader of the management team.

o: Calculates loads, forces and structural safety
questions.e: Calculates loads, forces and structural safety. Works alongside architect.
Principal Contractor manages the construction site and coordinates all subcontractors.

questions.q: What is the minimum burial depth for a cold water service pipe?
questions.e: 750mm minimum burial depth for frost protection.

questions.q: Maximum burial depth for a cold water service pipe?
questions.e: Maximum 1350mm. Minimum 750mm.

questions.q: Who is responsible for the communication pipe?
questions.e: Communication pipe (from main to boundary stopcock) = water undertaker's responsibility.

questions.q: Who is responsible for the service pipe?
questions.e: Service pipe (from boundary stopcock into building) = homeowner's responsibility.

questions.q: Minimum pipe size to a modern domestic property?
questions.e: 25mm MDPE blue minimum to modern properties.
Category 1 = wholesome water direct from mains. No backflow protection required.
Category 3 = slight health hazard. Bath, basin, shower water.
Category 5 = serious health hazard containing pathogens. WC, bidet, urinal, medical equipment.
Category 5 = air gap only. No mechanical device is sufficient.

questions.q: Protection against Category 3 fluid (e.g. bath water)?
questions.e: Category 3 = double check valve required.

questions.q: Outside tap (hose union bib tap) — backflow protection required?
questions.e: Double check valve fitted internally (for frost protection) on all hose union bib taps.

questions.q: RPZ valve — what makes it different from a double check valve?
o: Has a dump chamber between the two check valves — discharges to drain if first check fails
questions.e: RPZ = reduced pressure zone. Dump chamber between checks discharges to drain if first check fails.

questions.q: Ascending spray bidet — what must it NOT be connected to?
o: Combination boiler or unvented cylinder — no air gap
questions.e: Ascending bidet must not be connected to combi boiler or unvented cylinder — no air gap exists.

o: All water potable at all outlets; simple pipework; works with combi and unvented
questions.e: All outlets wholesome, mains pressure. Works well with combination boilers and unvented cylinders.

questions.q: Indirect cold water system — only wholesome outlet?
o: Kitchen sink only — fed directly from rising main
questions.e: Only the kitchen sink is fed directly from the mains. All other cold outlets come from the storage cistern.

questions.q: Minimum capacity of a cold water storage cistern feeding hot water cylinder only?
questions.e: 115 litres minimum if feeding cylinder only.

questions.q: Cold water storage cistern feeding hot AND cold distribution — minimum capacity?
questions.e: 230 litres minimum if feeding both hot and cold distribution.

questions.q: Cold water storage cistern — why insulate sides and top but NOT the underside?
o: Allow warmth from below to help prevent freezing
questions.e: Leave underside uninsulated to allow rising warmth from the building to help prevent freezing.

questions.q: BS 1212 Part 2 FOV — how is the water level adjusted?
o: Slide float up or down the vertical arm
questions.e: BS 1212 Part 2 — adjust by sliding the float up or down the vertical arm.

questions.q: BS 1212 Part 3 FOV — where is it typically used?
o: Toilet cisterns — achieves air gap as water fills from top
questions.e: Part 3 = plastic, toilet cisterns. Water fills from top creating an air gap (AG).

questions.q: BS 1212 Part 4 FOV — what is its key feature?
o: Very short arm; pressure equalises both sides; slow quiet shut-off prevents water hammer
questions.e: Equilibrium valve. Very short arm (~100mm). Slow quiet shut-off. Use to cure ball murmur.

questions.q: Cold feed to cylinder vs cold distribution — which connects higher in the cistern?
o: Cold feed connects 25mm higher than cold distribution
questions.e: Cold feed 25mm higher than cold distribution — ensures cold distribution empties before hot feed. Prevents scalding.

questions.q: Stagnation prevention in a cold water storage cistern?
o: Position primary draw-off directly opposite the FOV inlet to ensure all water circulates
questions.e: Draw-off opposite FOV forces all stored water to circulate before being drawn off.

questions.q: Warning pipe vs overflow pipe — difference?
o: Warning pipe is smaller — acts as early warning to customer; overflow is larger for higher volume discharge
questions.e: Warning pipe = smaller, discharges first as customer warning. Overflow = larger, for higher volume if warning pipe fails to alert.

questions.q: Over 1000 litre cistern — warning and overflow pipe requirement?
o: Two separate warning and overflow pipes required
questions.e: Over 1000 litres requires separate warning AND overflow pipes.

questions.q: When drilling a cold water storage cistern?
o: Use a heated copper pipe to melt through
Use a hole saw — never heat
questions.e: Always use a hole saw. Never apply heat — breaks down the polymer. Remove all swarf before installation.

questions.q: What jointing compound must NOT be used on plastic cisterns?
o: Boss Blue (contains linseed oil — degrades polymer)
questions.e: Boss Blue contains linseed oil which degrades plastic. No oil-based compounds on plastic cisterns.

o: WC flushing, garden irrigation, vehicle washing
Drinking water only if UV treated
questions.e: Grey water (baths, showers, basins, WMs) suitable for WC flushing, irrigation, vehicle washing — not drinking or personal washing.

questions.q: Rainwater harvesting — what is a first-flush diverter?
o: Diverts first flush of rain away from storage as it carries most contamination
questions.e: First-flush diverter diverts the initial rainfall which carries the most roof contamination away from the storage tank.

questions.q: Water treatment — what does sedimentation do?
o: Allows suspended particles to sink under gravity, sometimes aided by coagulants
questions.e: Sedimentation = suspended particles settle. Coagulants (e.g. aluminium sulfate) cause fine particles to flocculate and settle faster.

questions.q: Slow sand filter vs rapid sand filter?
o: Slow sand has a biological layer (schmutzdecke) and very low flow rate; rapid sand uses backwashing
Slow sand uses chemicals; rapid does not
questions.e: Slow sand = biological layer, ~0.1–0.3 m/hr. Rapid sand = physical straining, backwashed, ~5–15 m/hr.

questions.q: Water sterilisation — most common method for mains supply?
o: Chlorination — residual maintained through distribution network
questions.e: Chlorination most common. Residual chlorine maintained through distribution to protect against contamination.

o: Remove dissolved gases improving taste; oxidise iron and manganese for filtration
questions.e: Removes dissolved gases (CO₂, H₂S) improving taste. Oxidises iron/manganese so they can be filtered out.

questions.q: Break cistern in a boosted system — purpose?
o: Provides break between mains and pump to prevent negative pressure on mains
questions.e: Break cistern prevents pump suction creating negative pressure on the mains supply — backflow risk.

questions.q: Accumulator (pressure vessel) in a boosted system — purpose?
o: Stores compressed air to maintain pressure between pump cycles — reduces pump wear
Acts as expansion vessel for heating
questions.e: Compressed air bladder maintains system pressure between pump cycles. Reduces pump cycling and wear.

questions.q: Float switch in a pumped cold water system — function?
o: Controls pump based on cistern water level — starts pump at low level, stops at high level
questions.e: Float switch starts pump when level drops to low set point, stops at high set point.

questions.q: Submersible pump — where is it installed?
o: Below water level inside a borehole or tank — no priming required
questions.e: Below water level in borehole or tank. Motor sealed and cooled by water. No priming required.

questions.q: Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 — what five areas do they cover?
o: Waste, undue consumption, misuse, contamination, erroneous measurement
questions.e: The five areas: waste, undue consumption, misuse, contamination, erroneous measurement.

questions.q: Private Water Supplies Regulations 2009 — who is responsible for water quality?
questions.e: Private supply (borehole, spring, well) — property owner responsible for testing and quality.

questions.q: Pressure test for cold water — what instrument is used?
questions.e: Pressure gauge used at point of entry (e.g. outside tap position).

questions.q: Flow rate test — what instrument is used?
o: Weir cup or flow cup — measured in litres per minute
questions.e: Weir cup or flow cup. Measured in litres per minute.

questions.q: Drain-off valve (DOV) — where is it required?
o: Immediately above the internal stopcock and at every low point in the system
questions.e: DOV required immediately above internal stopcock and at every low point in the system.
Legionella grows between 20–46°C. Killed above 47°C. Store hot water above 60°C.

questions.q: Cold water storage should be kept below?
questions.e: Keep cold stored water below 20°C to prevent Legionella growth.

questions.q: Passive dead leg — what risk does it present?
o: Stagnant water at warm temperature — Legionella risk
questions.e: Capped-off pipe full of stagnant water is a Legionella risk. Cut back to the branch immediately.

questions.q: Air gap AUK2 — minimum dimension for a basin?
questions.e: AUK2 = basin spillover to tap spout minimum 20mm. Bath minimum 25mm.

questions.q: Air gap AUK3 (kitchen sink) — why larger than AUK2?
o: Kitchen sink is Category 5 — requires larger air gap (minimum 2× bore of supply pipe)
questions.e: Kitchen sink is Category 5 (food preparation). Requires minimum air gap of 2× the bore of the supply pipe.
